title: Diagnosis and Management of Endocrine Hypertension: Deciding Who to Screen for Genetic Causes
authors: William F. Young, Jr., MD, MSc.

items.question: You see two 50-year-old women with adrenal incidentalomas. Which patient(s) should be screened for pheochromocytoma?
items.explanation: An adrenal incidentaloma with an unenhanced CT attenuation of less than 10 HU cannot be a pheochromocytoma, thus avoiding false-positive biochemical testing for Patient 1.
items.reference: MAIN CONCLUSIONS: "In a multicenter retrospective study of 533 patients with 548 histologically confirmed pheochromocytomas...374 had an attenuation value greater than 10 HU (99.5%)."

items.question: Why is it important to screen for primary aldosteronism in patients with hypertension?
items.answer: Because the prevalence of target-organ damage and cardiovascular toxicity extends beyond hypertension, leading to increased morbidity.
keyPoints: Increased risk of stroke, coronary artery disease, atrial fibrillation, heart failure, diabetes, metabolic syndrome, and left ventricular hypertrophy in patients with primary aldosteronism compared to essential hypertension.
items.reference: MAIN CONCLUSIONS: "The cardiovascular toxicity in primary aldosteronism extends beyond hypertension; there is an aldosterone-specific toxicity."

items.question: How should genetic testing be approached in patients with pheochromocytoma?
items.answer: Genetic testing is recommended, especially if the patient has a family history of pheochromocytoma or related syndromic disorders.
keyPoints: Consideration for all patients with pheochromocytoma
Particularly important in cases with bilateral adrenal involvement, young age at diagnosis, or familial syndromes.
items.reference: MAIN CONCLUSIONS: "Genetic testing should be considered in all patients with pheochromocytoma, especially if a patient has 1 or more of the following..."
